design_philosophy.core_loop: Select commander → Allocate quarterly budget across navy/districts/expenses → Manage district actions (train, bribe, parade) → Resolve tactical hex-grid battles → Advance calendar by half-month turns
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Political pressure from Congress/Parliament (who control budget) vs. military necessity; Americans must survive and strengthen while British must fortify and attack quickly
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command-in-Chief of Revolutionary War forces, making strategic decisions about resource allocation, district management, and large-scale military campaigns with historical authenticity
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Extreme complexity and historical detail as core design philosophy; rejection of player-friendly accessibility in favor of simulation depth; political/civilian oversight mechanics that constrain military freedom; asymmetric win conditions for each side reflecting historical asymmetry

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can choose which Commander-in-Chief to control (affecting starting conditions and available generals), and can set overall naval strategy; difficulty is inherent to the game system rather than selectable

progression.upgrade_system: Training troops in districts; purchasing materials; bribing enemy generals; holding parades for popular support; promoting commanders through ranks
progression.unlock_mechanism: Militia regiments (Patriot/Loyalist or Mercenary) auto-organize and can be recruited by regular commanders; naval forces operate semi-autonomously until directed
replayability_hooks: Six different Commanders-in-Chief with different strategic contexts
Asymmetric gameplay between American (defensive, slow growth) and British (fortify and attack) sides
Two-player mode and zero-player AI vs AI mode
Extreme game length (250+ hours) encourages multiple playthroughs across campaigns

world_building_techniques: Seasonal terrain changes on hex-grid battlefields
Political entities (Continental Congress, British Parliament) as mechanical and narrative constraints
District control representing territorial control narrative

faction_indicators: Patriot militia vs Loyalist militia regiments (ideological split materialized mechanically)
American Continental Army vs British Army command structures
danger_signals: Loss of Congressional/Parliamentary approval can result in player ouster from command
Budget restrictions from civilian oversight can starve military operations
Militia regiments operating autonomously can make unpredictable territorial decisions
environmental_storytelling: Thirteen Colonies map with districts showing control state
Fort and town placement reflects historical strategic importance
Naval forces stationed at coastal locations
Terrain types and seasonal changes affect tactical possibilities
